title: VPN for Remote Work: Why It Matters and What to Look For in 2026
description: Remote workers handle sensitive data on home networks and public connections. A VPN adds a layer of protection that company firewalls do not. Here is what to consider.
content:
Why Remote Workers Need a VPN

Working from home or a cafe means your traffic travels over networks you do not control. Your internet service provider can log the sites you visit. On public Wi-Fi, other users on the same network can intercept unencrypted traffic. A VPN encrypts your connection from your device to the VPN server, removing both of these risks.

For employees handling client data, financial records, or any information covered by GDPR, HIPAA, or other regulations, unencrypted connections are a compliance risk, not just a personal one.

What to Look for in a Remote Work VPN

No-logs policy: Verify that the VPN provider has an audited no-logs policy. This means the provider does not store records of which sites you visit or when you connect. NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad have all completed independent audits confirming their no-logs claims.

Kill switch: A kill switch cuts your internet connection if the VPN drops unexpectedly. Without it, your real IP and traffic are briefly exposed when the VPN reconnects. For work purposes, a kill switch is not optional.

Split tunneling: This feature lets you route only some traffic through the VPN. For example, you can send company software through the VPN while streaming video on your normal connection. This avoids performance slowdowns from routing all traffic through an encrypted tunnel.

Multi-device support: Most people work across a laptop, phone, and sometimes a tablet. Check how many simultaneous connections the plan allows. Five or more is standard with most paid providers.

Company VPN vs. Personal VPN

If your employer provides a company VPN, use it for company systems. A personal VPN adds privacy on top of that for personal browsing. They serve different purposes and can run simultaneously using split tunneling.

Performance Considerations

A VPN always adds some latency because your traffic takes an extra hop through the VPN server. To minimize this: choose a server geographically close to you, use the WireGuard protocol where available (it is faster than OpenVPN), and avoid free VPNs that throttle bandwidth or overcrowd their servers.
